subject: ap-calculus-ab
title: Two Particles — Velocity, Acceleration, Position & End Behavior
stimulus: Particle $P$ moves along the $x$-axis such that, for time $t > 0$, its position is given by $x_P(t) = 6 - 4e^{-t}$. Particle $Q$ moves along the $y$-axis such that, for time $t > 0$, its velocity is given by $v_Q(t) = \frac{1}{t^2}$. At time $t = 1$, the position of particle $Q$ is $y_Q(1) = 2$.

parts.prompt: Find $v_P(t)$, the velocity of particle $P$ at time $t$.
parts.scoring_notes: Must differentiate $x_P(t)$. A response equating $x_P(t)$ with $v_P(t)$ does not earn the point.

parts.prompt: Find $a_Q(t)$, the acceleration of particle $Q$ at time $t$. Find all times $t$, for $t > 0$, when the speed of particle $Q$ is decreasing. Justify your answer.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Speed is decreasing for all $t > 0$ because velocity and acceleration have opposite signs
parts.scoring_notes: Second point: must consider signs of both $v_Q(t)$ and $a_Q(t)$. Third point: answer must be consistent with presented justification. A response indicating $v_Q(t) < 0$ does not earn the third point.

parts.prompt: Find $y_Q(t)$, the position of particle $Q$ at time $t$.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Correct integral setup: $y_Q(t) = y_Q(1) + \int_1^t v_Q(s)\, ds = 2 + \int_1^t \frac{1}{s^2}\, ds$
parts.scoring_notes: Integral point earned for presenting $\int \frac{1}{t^2}\, dt$ or $-\frac{1}{t}$ (with no integral). A response of $y_Q(t) = -\frac{1}{t} + 3$ earns all three points. $y_Q(t) = -\frac{1}{t} + C$ with $C \ne 3$ earns only first point.

parts.prompt: As $t \to \infty$, which particle will eventually be farther from the origin? Give a reason for your answer.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: One correct limit: $\lim_{t\to\infty} x_P(t) = \lim_{t\to\infty}(6 - 4e^{-t}) = 6$ and $\lim_{t\to\infty} y_Q(t) = \lim_{t\to\infty}\left(3 - \frac{1}{t}\right) = 3$
1 pt: Particle $P$ will eventually be farther from the origin because $6 > 3$
parts.scoring_notes: Must present at least one correct limit. Statements such as '$x_P(t)$ approaches $6$' earn first point. A response that treats $\infty$ as an input (e.g., $6 - 4e^{-\infty}$) is not eligible for second point.
